content:
First observe that any block of 1’s of length \(k\) represents the “repunit”  
\[
R_k = 11\ldots1\;(k\text{ times}) = \frac{10^k-1}{9}.
\]  
Since \(1111>1000\), the only repunits \(\le1000\) are  
\[
R_1=1,\;R_2=11,\;R_3=111.
\]  
We look for non‐negative integers \(x,y,z\) with
\[
x\cdot1 \;+\; y\cdot11 \;+\; z\cdot111 \;=\;1000.
\]
For each solution \((x,y,z)\), the total number of 1’s is
\[
n \;=\; x\cdot1 + y\cdot2 + z\cdot3.
\]
Writing the equation as
\[
x + 11y +111z=1000
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x=1000-11y-111z,
\]
one finds
\[
n= (1000-11y-111z)+2y+3z \;=\;1000-9y-108z.
\]
Hence all possible \(n\)’s are of the form
\[
n=1000-9y-108z,
\]
where \(z=0,1,\dots,9\) and \(0\le y\le\big\lfloor(1000-111z)/11\big\rfloor\).

If we set
\[
n=1+9t,
\]
one checks by a short “interval‐union” argument that the attainable \(t\) are
\[
\{\,3\}\;\cup\;\{5,6,7,\dots,111\},
\]
i.e.\ all integers from 3 up to 111 except \(t=4\).  That is
\[
\bigl|\{\,t\}\bigr| = 1 + (111-5+1)=108.
\]
Hence there are \(a=108\) different \(n\) for which the sum can be made \(1000\).  Finally
\[
\text{sum of digits of }a = 1+0+8 = 9.
\]
\boxed{9}
